mnemonics.title: Strict Aliasing Rule
mnemonics.content: SAR: Strict Aliasing Rules - Only char* can alias. Remember: 'Char Can Access Anything' - all other types must stand alone.

mnemonics.title: Restrict Keyword Purpose
mnemonics.content: RESTRICT: Reassures Compiler No Aliasing, Enables Safe Transformations. Think: 'My pointer is RESTRICTed to this memory only'.

mnemonics.title: Volatile Usage
mnemonics.content: VIO: Volatile Indicates Outside changes. Remember: 'V' for 'Various' - value can change unexpectedly from hardware or other sources.

mnemonics.title: Memcpy vs Memmove
mnemonics.content: MEM: Move with Extra buffer handles overlap. Think: 'memmove' has an 'extra' letter for 'extra' safety with overlapping memory.

acronyms.usage: Rule in C that only allows char* pointers to alias other types
Compiler hint that pointer is the only reference to the object
Indicates value may change unexpectedly without notice
Pointer value that doesn't point to valid memory (typically address 0)
Hardware registers in microcontrollers often accessed via pointers

visualTips.visualization: Draw two arrows (pointers) pointing to the same box (memory location). Label one 'ptr1' and another 'ptr2' to show both access same data.
Show a box labeled 'int' with an 'int*' arrow, but also show a 'float*' arrow pointing to same box - draw an X through this as forbidden.
Draw a box with only ONE arrow labeled 'restrict int* ptr' - draw a barrier showing no other pointers can enter.
Draw a hardware register box with an external arrow labeled 'Hardware' showing it can change the value anytime, plus a 'volatile int*' arrow from software.
Draw a union as a single box with two labels: 'int' and 'float' - show writing to int side and reading from float side in same box.

analogies.analogy: Like two people having keys to the same house - changes made by one (locking door) affect what the other sees (can't enter).
Like each room in an apartment building only having one specific key type - you can't use a car key to open a bedroom door.
Like hiring an exclusive personal assistant who only handles your schedule - no one else has access to make changes.
Like a social media notification - it can change at any moment without you doing anything, so you always need to check the current state.
memcpy is like copying files to a new folder assuming they don't overlap. memmove is like using a temp folder first - safer but slightly slower.
